Win Probability, Not Just Accuracy

The ABS Challenge Leaderboard We Actually Wanted

Overturn rate tells you who was right. xWPA asks whether being right mattered.

Updated 2026-05-03 Regular season ABS challenges through 2026-05-03.
Challenges Studied1,928
Overturn Rate53.4%
League Direct xWPA+1851.4
Top ClubCOL
Advertisement

There are two stories inside every ABS challenge. The first is the one the broadcast can resolve instantly: did the strike-zone graphic move the call from red to green? The second is slower and much more interesting: what did that correction do to the game?

The public leaderboard that inspired this project is a useful start. Overturn rate is tidy, and overturn rate above expectation adds needed context. But a sixth-inning 0-0 miss and a ninth-inning full-count pitch with the bases loaded should not live in the same accounting bucket. A challenge is a tiny replay review attached to a huge amount of game state.

So this version scores every challenge by expected win probability added. For each pitch, the script reconstructs the inning, half-inning, score, base/out state, count, batting team, and fielding team from MLB Stats API game feeds. It then asks a simple counterfactual question: what was the challenging team's win probability after the original umpire call, and what was it after the ABS-corrected call?

Through 2026-05-03, the top team is Colorado Rockies (COL) at +1.058 direct challenge wins, or +105.8 WPA points.

The early leaderboard has a pleasingly weird shape. The first-place club is Colorado Rockies (COL), but the story is not just volume. The leader's challenges have produced +1.2 points per attempt, and the risk-adjusted leader is San Diego Padres (SD) once the failed-challenge inventory proxy is included. The league as a whole has banked +18.514 direct wins of call-correction value before accounting for the cost of unsuccessful challenges.

The single largest swing in the file is the kind of pitch that makes this exercise feel necessary. 2026-04-16: Edgar Quero (CWS) turned a Top 9, 3-2 count with 1B2B3B into a Ball to Strike challenge worth +38.1 WPA points. That one pitch carries more scoreboard weight than a dozen low-leverage confirms.

Leaderboard

The Team Board Changes When the Stakes Change

Separating the Call From the Decision

The cleanest number here is direct xWPA. It gives full credit when ABS flips the call and zero direct credit when the call stands. That keeps the metric tied to the thing that actually changed on the field.

The risk-adjusted column is a second lens, not a replacement. Failed challenges matter because teams can run out of them, so the script converts Savant's lost-challenge run value into wins using a 10-runs-per-win rule of thumb. That is a practical proxy for the hidden option value of keeping a challenge available. It is intentionally labeled as a proxy because MLB has not published an official challenge-inventory win model.

The Challenge That Never Came

There is now a third bucket: missed opportunities. These are not observed decisions, so they should be handled with more humility. The model scans every called ball and called strike in the MLB game feed, compares the pitch location to an inferred ABS rectangle, estimates the chance that a challenge would have flipped the call from the pitch's distance beyond the edge, then weighs that against the game's win-probability leverage and the team's remaining challenge inventory.

Through 2026-05-03, the model finds 1,428 positive expected-value pitches that were not challenged, worth +1253.3 expected WPA points left on the table. The biggest is this one: 2026-04-21: Orion Kerkering (PHI) passed on a Bot 5, 3-2 ball with 1B2B3B; the model had it 0.9 inches over the inferred ABS edge and worth +11.4 expected points if challenged to strike.

Shape of the Season

Volume Helps, But Leverage Is the Cheat Code

The Reverse Leaderboard

There is another skill hiding in the data. When hitters challenge a called strike and lose, the catcher and pitcher have helped turn conviction into a burned challenge. The stat here is called failed challenges against. It is credited only on hitter-initiated challenges that were confirmed, and its value column, fooled xWPA, gives positive credit for the opponent's lost-challenge proxy.

On that board, Cal Raleigh, SEA has been the early standout among catchers with 18 failed hitter challenges against. Among pitchers, Tim Mayza, PHI leads at 6. This is not a pure framing stat, and it is not meant to be one. It is closer to a game-theory stat: who is living in the hitter's head long enough to make the hitter spend?

Estimated Misses

Who Creates, and Who Baits the Miss?

The Plays

The Highest-Leverage Challenges So Far

How It Works

  • Challenge attempts come from Baseball Savant's ABS leaderboard and challenge drawer service.
  • Each pitch is joined to MLB Stats API live game feeds by game and pitch id.
  • The win-probability model is trained from completed 2026 regular-season game feeds through 2026-05-03.
  • For each challenge, the model evaluates the original call and the ABS-corrected call from the challenging team's perspective.
  • Risk-adjusted xWPA adds a failed-challenge inventory proxy based on Savant's lost-challenge run value, converted at 10 runs per win.
  • Missed opportunities are inferred from MLB game-feed pitch coordinates, a strike-zone rectangle expanded by a baseball radius, game leverage, modeled overturn probability from distance beyond the edge, and remaining challenge-loss inventory.

Caveats

  • This is an independent model, not an official MLB or Statcast metric.
  • Early-season run distributions are noisy, especially in rare count/base/out states.
  • The inventory penalty is a proxy. A full version would model challenge availability, inning rules, and future challenge quality inside each game.
  • Player credit follows the challenge side: hitter for batting challenges; catcher, pitcher, and fielding challenger for fielding challenges.
  • Missed-opportunity player credit is estimated only for hitters on missed batting challenges and pitchers on missed fielding challenges because the public game feed does not reliably expose the catcher on every historical pitch.

Sources: MLB.com on ABS challenge skill, Baseball Savant ABS leaderboard, and MLB Stats API game feeds.

Built as a static page from the same daily pipeline that writes the CSVs. The interactive graphics use embedded data, so the page can be published as ordinary static HTML.
